title: TabLLM: Few-shot Classification of Tabular Data with Large Language Models
authors: Stefan Hegselmann, Alejandro Buendia, Hunter Lang, Monica Agrawal, Xiaoyi Jiang, David Sontag (MIT CSAIL; University of Münster)
tokenization_method: TEXT SERIALIZATION + LLM subword tokenizer. There is no learned per-feature tabular tokenizer at all: a table ROW is converted into a natural-language string by a serialize(F, x) function over column names F and values x, then tokenized by the LLM's own (SentencePiece) tokenizer and concatenated with a fixed task prompt p (e.g. 'Does this person earn more than 50000 dollars? Yes or no? Answer:'). Predictions come from the normalized probabilities the LLM assigns to a manually-specified VERBALIZER token set (e.g. 'Yes'/'No') mapped to class labels. Nine serialization variants are studied, grouped as: (a) MANUAL templates — List Template ('column: value' list with a fixed column order) and Text Template ('The <column name> is <value>.' enumerated as sentences); (b) LLM-GENERATED text — Table-To-Text (Narrativaai/bloom-560m TOTTO model, fed one column-value tuple at a time and concatenated), Text-T0 (T0pp 11B fed two column-value pairs at a time with 'Write this information as a sentence:'), Text-GPT-3 (text-davinci-002 fed the whole list with 'Rewrite all list items in the input as a natural text.'); (c) ABLATION serializations — List Only Values (drop column names), List Permuted Names (associate the wrong column name with each value, same permutation across rows), List Permuted Values (per-column value permutation, continuous values pre-binned into 10 uniform bins), List Short (cap at 10 features, healthcare only). Headline finding: the simple manual Text Template wins for zero/very-few shots; sophisticated LLM serializers UNDERPERFORM it (they hallucinate / drop features). Few-shot adaptation is parameter-efficient fine-tuning via the T-Few recipe (Liu et al. 2022) on the k labeled, serialized examples.
feature_types_handled: numerical, categorical (including the healthcare claims set's medical concepts), and missing — all flattened into one text string. Numerical and categorical are NOT distinguished by the tokenizer; both become words/numbers in a sentence. Missing values: handled implicitly by serialization choice (a feature can simply be omitted from the sentence). Temporal: only ad-hoc — the healthcare claims data is a time series of visits, manually serialized as a chronological prose narrative ('It then describes each visit, stating its date, the type of doctor...'), i.e. time is encoded as English text, not a structured temporal token.
token_granularity: subword tokens of a natural-language sentence (LLM/SentencePiece BPE). One tabular 'token' has no fixed correspondence to one feature — a single numerical value like 2174 may split into several subwords, and a column name like 'capital gain' spans multiple tokens. This is the OPPOSITE of FT-Transformer's per-feature token: granularity is whatever the text tokenizer produces, and total sequence length grows with the verbosity of the serialization, bounded hard by T0's 1024-token (~400-word) limit.
numerical_strategy: raw numeric value written as a DIGIT STRING inside the sentence (e.g. 'The gain is 594.'); no binning, scaling, piecewise-linear, periodic, or quantile embedding. The LLM tokenizer splits the number into subwords. The List Permuted Values ablation bins continuous values into 10 uniform bins only to test reliance on fine-grained values, not as the production encoding. Authors explicitly concede in the conclusion that adding 'numeric-specific encoding methods (Gorishniy et al. 2022)' is future work — i.e. raw-digit serialization is the weak point.
categorical_strategy: the category's natural-language STRING is written verbatim into the sentence (e.g. 'has a Master's degree', occupation 'Farming-fishing'). Open-vocabulary: no per-category lookup table, one-hot, hashing, or target encoding on the TabLLM side. Some values were manually mapped to more human-readable forms from dataset docs (e.g. workclass 'private' -> 'private sector employee') to better match the LLM's pretraining distribution. Semantics ride entirely on the LLM's pretrained word embeddings.
high_cardinality_handling: no dedicated mechanism, and high cardinality interacts badly with the token budget. Because each value is spelled out as text, many distinct categories cost tokens but not parameters; however the real bottleneck is the 1024-token limit. On the 106-feature healthcare claims dataset TabLLM could NOT fit all features and had to (a) select the most-frequent medical concepts per patient and (b) use List Short (<=10 features) — i.e. it drops information rather than encoding high cardinality efficiently. So high-cardinality / many-feature handling is by lossy truncation, not by a scalable encoder.
temporal_handling: no structured temporal tokenization. The only temporal case (healthcare claims) is a per-visit prose narrative with explicit dates written in English; ordering and recency are conveyed as text and via three engineered indicator windows (within 30 days / 1 year / anytime) in the BASELINE one-hot encoding, not in TabLLM's serialization. No patching, recurrence, or channel-as-token.
scales_to_many_features: WEAK / negative evidence for the topic's 70+-feature setting. Public benchmark datasets were deliberately capped at <=30 columns precisely to stay within T0's 1024-token limit (and <=50k rows for fine-tune cost). The one many-feature case (106-feature claims data) required dropping features (concept selection + List Short<=10) to fit, and there LR/LightGBM overtook TabLLM beyond 64-256 shots, which the authors attribute to 'information lost from our concept selection procedure, needed because of the token limits'. Sequence length scales linearly with feature verbosity and is hard-capped, so naive serialization does NOT scale to 70+ rich features without truncation; compute footprint is also far larger than XGBoost/LR (needs 'fairly large GPUs').
downstream_model: an LLM consumes the tokens directly. Primary: T0 / T0pp encoder-decoder, 11B params (also T0 3B, slightly worse), 1024-token limit. Zero-shot comparison also with GPT-3 text-davinci-002 (175B). The LLM IS the classifier (no separate head): class probability = normalized probability of generating the verbalizer token sequence.
pretraining_objective: none added by TabLLM. It is pure transfer from the off-the-shelf LLM's existing pretraining (T0's multitask prompted training; GPT-3's web-scale LM). Few-shot adaptation = T-Few parameter-efficient fine-tuning (learned (IA)^3-style scaling vectors + losses) on the k serialized labeled examples, default T-Few hyperparameters, ~30 epochs, no per-task tuning.
key_results: Evaluated on 9 public datasets (Bank, Blood, Calhousing, Car, Credit-g, Diabetes, Heart, Income, Jungle) + 3 private health-claims tasks (EoL, Surgery, LoH), metric = AUC, 5 seeds. Zero-shot is non-trivial on most datasets (e.g. Income 0.84, Car 0.82 AUC at 0 shots) — exploiting LLM prior knowledge; on par with the 175B GPT-3 despite T0 being 11B. TabLLM (T0 + Text Template) matches or beats the GBDT baselines (XGBoost/LightGBM) up to 256 shots on all public datasets except Calhousing and Jungle, and beats SAINT/NODE/TabNet in many settings; in the very-few-shot regime it wins by 'over 5 AUC points'. On full data it is on par or close to the best baselines (little lost to serialization). STRONGEST baseline overall is TabPFN, which consistently matches/exceeds TabLLM and reaches TabLLM's level with 4-256 shots. On the 106-feature healthcare claims data TabLLM beats LR up to 16-64 shots and LightGBM up to 64 (LoH) / 256 (EoL) shots, then is overtaken. Feature-importance introspection on Income shows TabLLM's top-5 weighted features overlap the top-7 of an all-data LR; on EoL its top-5 zero-shot features all have significantly elevated relative risk.
baselines_compared: Logistic Regression (LR, extensively tuned), XGBoost, LightGBM, TabNet, SAINT, NODE, TabPFN, and zero-shot GPT-3 (text-davinci-002). Categoricals one-hot for baselines (ordinal tested, worse). 4-fold CV on the k-shots for tuning (deliberately NO large validation set, to keep it truly few-shot). Healthcare: only LR and LightGBM (runtime).
transferable_idea: Use the natural-language semantics of COLUMN NAMES + VALUES as the encoding, so a pretrained LLM contributes zero-shot/few-shot prior knowledge with no learned tabular tokenizer and no per-task feature engineering — and confirm empirically (via the permuted-names / only-values / permuted-values ablations) that the model genuinely uses (i) correct feature names and (ii) correct fine-grained values in the very-few-shot regime, with that dependence vanishing by ~256 shots. For the 70+-feature topic, the reusable nugget is the column-name-as-semantics idea (shared with CARTE) and the ablation protocol for proving a tokenizer uses names/values; the verbatim text-serialization mechanism itself is NOT directly reusable at 70+ rich features because of the token-budget ceiling.
limitations: Authors-stated: (1) much larger compute footprint than XGBoost/LR — needs large GPUs to fine-tune, inference is far more FLOPs. (2) Token-limit ceiling: dense/many-feature rows that exceed the LLM context lose features (caused the healthcare degradation past 256 shots). (3) Gains require the column-name/value semantics to have appeared in the LLM's PRETRAINING — fails on opaque names (e.g. gene IDs) or specialized domains; T0 had little medical knowledge, hurting medical tasks. (4) Dataset shift: pretraining corpus may not reflect the table's aggregation context (e.g. inflation changing the meaning of dollar amounts). (5) LLM serializers hallucinate / drop features, so they underperform manual templates. (6) Uses no unlabeled data.
pitfalls: 1) TOKEN-BUDGET SCALING WALL (topic-critical): public datasets were capped at <=30 columns by design and the 106-feature case needed lossy concept-selection + List Short<=10 to fit T0's 1024-token limit; verbatim text serialization does NOT scale to 70+ rich features without dropping information — treat any 'works on tabular data' claim as <=30 readable columns. 2) The few-shot win is concentrated in the VERY-few-shot regime (<=64-256 shots) and on datasets with semantically meaningful textual values (Income, Car); on numeric/medical datasets and beyond a few hundred shots, GBDTs / LR / TabPFN catch up or win (proposal's GBDT-vs-deep caveat applies). 3) The strongest baseline is TabPFN, not a tree — and TabPFN beats TabLLM across most shot counts, so 'LLM beats deep tabular' should not be over-read. 4) Numerical encoding is raw digit-string text with no numeric-specific embedding (authors flag adding Gorishniy 2022 as future work); do not import this as a numerical-tokenization recipe. 5) Reported few-shot numbers deliberately avoid a large validation set, so XGBoost's weak very-few-shot AUC partly reflects no tuning headroom, not a fundamental gap. 6) Conflicts partly with LIFT/Dinh 2022, which on GPT-3 could NOT confirm column-name importance at fractional-data settings and found fine-tuned GPT-3 worse than LR up to 250 shots — sample-efficiency is highly task- and LLM-dependent.
relevance_to_topic: Canonical representative of the llm-tabular-serialization route: shows you can tokenize a heterogeneous mixed-type row with zero learned tabular tokenizer by spelling it out as text and leaning on LLM priors — strong in the very-few-shot regime — but its hard 1024-token ceiling, lossy truncation on the 106-feature case, raw-digit numerics, and heavy compute make it a cautionary anchor, not a recipe, for the 70+-feature target setting.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/clinicalml/TabLLM (serialization templates, T-Few fine-tuning scripts; public-dataset experiments reproducible, healthcare claims data private).
